title: Chapter One: The Open Gate
content:
> *Case 7, Xinye County Death Registry, Ninth Year of the Yongle Emperor: "The child born under the open crown will not see his twentieth winter. I have witnessed this condition only once before, in a monk of the Western Reaches who dissolved into light at the age of seventeen. The Baihui was a door left ajar, and the heavens walked through it. There is no medicine for a man whose skull cannot keep his soul inside." -- Attributed to a wandering physician, identity unrecorded. Provenance disputed.*

---

Moonlight touched the surface of the river like a pale hand reaching into dark water.

The man who knocked on the gate of the Jeong estate did not look like he belonged to this province, or this century, or perhaps this world at all. His ears were long -- longer than a human's by the width of two fingers -- tapering to narrow points that caught the light like blades of grass. His face held a beauty that was not quite beauty, but something adjacent to it. Something that made the eye linger without knowing why.

He smelled of green leaves and old stone. These were not things a gatekeeper consciously noticed, but they were the reason old Zhang did not turn the stranger away.

"I am a physician," the man said. His voice was unhurried, as if he had been walking for a very long time and had nowhere particular left to go. "I would be grateful for a night's shelter. In exchange, I will treat any who are ill."

Zhang brought him to the master.

The master of the Jeong estate was a man named Jeong Dae-myeong, who had spent fifteen years building a martial family in Xinye County from the bones of his father's reputation. He was not a large man, but he filled rooms. He filled them with his voice, his expectations, and the particular weight of a man who had decided long ago that the world owed him something it had not yet delivered.

Tonight, however, Jeong Dae-myeong was distracted. His third wife had died in childbirth three days prior, and the infant she had left behind would not stop screaming.

"You are a physician," Dae-myeong said, studying the stranger's ears with an expression that mixed suspicion and awe in roughly equal measure. "Can you examine the child?"

The long-eared man took the baby. He held it with the practiced ease of someone who had held many children over many years -- perhaps more years than Dae-myeong could readily imagine. The infant quieted. It stared upward with dark, wet eyes, and the stranger stared back, and something passed between them that neither the father nor the gatekeeper could see.

"Ah," the stranger said. Just that. A single syllable, carrying the weight of a diagnosis.

"What is it?"

"It will be difficult for this child to live past twenty." The stranger's tone was clinical, stripped of sentiment. He might have been discussing the yield of a wheat field. "That is the constitution he was born with."

Dae-myeong went still. The kind of stillness that comes before a storm, or before a man decides whether to weep or strike something.

"An early death," he said. "This child was born after I forced myself on his mother. Is this divine punishment?"

The stranger's expression changed. Not to anger, precisely. Something closer to the look a scholar gives a student who has confused arithmetic with philosophy.

"Is that what you ask a doctor? You could at least pretend to be concerned with the medical reality." He turned the child gently in his arms, pressing two fingers to the soft crown of the infant's skull. "Even for an ignorant country lord, that is a remarkably foolish question."

"Watch your tongue. I have asked you about the child's condition, not for your judgment of my character."

"The condition is this." The stranger spoke over Dae-myeong's bluster as if it were birdsong -- present, ignorable. "You have heard of the Nine Yin Severed Pulse? Where the meridians harden and the body can no longer circulate qi?"

"I know of it. A death sentence."

"This is related but inverted. Your son's Baihui acupoint -- the gate at the crown of the head -- is abnormally open. Not closed, not hardened. Open. Wide open, like a window left unshuttered in a storm." He paused. "Do you understand what the Baihui connects to?"

"The upper dantian," Dae-myeong said. He was a martial artist. He knew this much.

"Yes. The seat of consciousness. The place where a cultivator's spirit resides. In your son, this gate is so wide that the natural energy of heaven and earth pours through it unchecked. Most cultivators spend decades trying to open the Baihui even a fraction. This child was born with it gaping."

"Then he should be a prodigy."

"He should be dead already." The stranger looked down at the child again. His expression softened by a fraction -- the barest human concession to pity. "The energy that enters through the crown must be processed by the upper dantian. If the gate is too wide, the energy overwhelms the spirit. The common name for this death is ascension."

"Ascension?" Dae-myeong frowned. "That is what they call it when a Taoist master of supreme cultivation departs for the celestial realm. The scriptures say --"

"The scriptures romanticize it. Ascension is what happens when a man's soul is drowned by the energy of nature. The spirit dissolves. The body follows. It is not a journey to heaven. It is an erasure." The stranger placed the baby back into its crib with a gentleness that contradicted everything about his sharp tone. "Your son will not ascend in glory. He will simply... disperse. Slowly, over the course of his short life, unless something changes."

Dae-myeong stared at the child. Then at the stranger. Then at the child again.

"It is divine punishment," he said quietly.

The stranger's lip curled. "You turtle of a man. To think you call yourself a father."

"I have heard enough." Dae-myeong rose, and his voice rose with him. "You are from the murim -- the martial world. What is your rank? Even if you are of noble lineage, your words --"

"Rank?" The stranger laughed. It was not a kind laugh. "You dare speak of rank to me? If your great-grandfather's great-grandfather stood before me, I would still --"

He did not finish the sentence. Dae-myeong called for the servants, and the stranger was escorted out of the estate with more force than courtesy.

Their conversation did not travel beyond the walls.

---

The stranger walked alone down the riverbank, his long ears catching the moonlight. The infant's face stayed with him -- the dark eyes, the quiet intelligence already forming behind them, the crown of the skull where he could feel the torrent of heaven's energy rushing downward like a river through a broken dam.

"If raised in an unorthodox sect," he murmured to himself, "this child would have become a demon. If raised in Shaolin, the Seventy-Two Arts would have doubled. If raised in my own homeland..." He trailed off. That was a thought he chose not to finish.

He shook his head.

"But born as a hated child in such a backwater place, with no teacher and no guidance, there is no path but to die young in this countryside."

He walked a few more steps. The river whispered against the stones.

"Unless he awakens on his own."

The stranger considered the probability of this. It was vanishingly small. It was the kind of thing that happened once in a century, if it happened at all.

He kept walking.

---
